item.title: Multiobjective Optimization of LLC Resonant Converters for Efficiency and Power Density Based on Time Domain Analysis and Genetic Algorithm
item.description: This article presents an optimization design methodology for LLC resonant converters, aimed at optimizing both converter losses and the volume of magnetic components. The optimization variables include transformer turns ratio, resonant inductor, magnetizing inductor, resonant capacitor, and peak flux density. In rated power conditions, the converter operates in specified operational modes, which enable soft switching and ensure monotonic voltage gain. A time-domain model of the LLC converter is discussed, and a rolling iterative method is proposed for obtaining time-domain solutions across a wide voltage gain range. Based on time-domain solutions, the loss model can be accurately calculated, and the design of magnetic components is provided. Due to the inherent tradeoff between loss and volume, a single optimal solution cannot be determined. The Nondominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II is employed to optimize both loss and volume, seeking multiple Pareto-optimal solutions. Finally, experimental results demonstrate that the converter can achieve a maximum efficiency of 98% or a minimum magnetic component volume of 19.03 cm3.

item.title: A Reconfigurable Nonisolated Three-Port Converter With Hybrid Quadrilateral Current Modulation for Wide-Range Soft-Switching in PV-Storage Systems
item.description: Addressing limitations in conventional nonisolated three-port converters (TPCs) for photovoltaic-energy storage (PV-ES) systems—such as restricted voltage range, high hard-switching losses, and soft-switching performance dependent on load power, this article proposes a reconfigurable, high power density, lossless nonisolated TPC. By integrating a dual-output architecture with the concept of power-path reconfiguration, single-stage wide-voltage-range energy conversion among the PV port, ES port, and load port is achieved. Combining an inductor series mechanism and switch parallel capacitor design, a hybrid reconfigurable quadrilateral modulation method for inductor current is proposed. This enables TPC to achieve full-load-range zero-voltage switching (ZVS) across a wide input voltage range of 29.43–80 V, increasing the maximum power transfer capability to 512.99 W. Furthermore, aiming to minimize the root-mean-square inductor current, the optimal duty cycle combinations for achieving full-load-range soft-switching in multiple operating modes are determined, significantly reducing switch conduction losses and inductor losses. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed TPC achieves full-range ZVS in all three modes: single-input dual-output, dual-input single-output, and single-input single-output. It achieves a peak efficiency of 97.1% at rated power with voltage ripple below 0.71%. This work provides theoretical and technical support for efficient energy management in PV-ES systems.

item.title: Common-Mode Energy Distribution for Leakage Current Attenuation in Three-Level NPC Inverters
item.description: Transformerless photovoltaic (PV) inverters are susceptible to leakage current, which is primarily caused by common-mode voltage (CMV). Most modulation strategies for three-level inverters focus on reducing the amplitude variation of this voltage, but often overlook the impact of its frequency spectrum. Since leakage current flows through a series RLC path defined by the output filter and the parasitic capacitance of the PV panels, shifting the spectral energy of the CMV away from the resonance frequency contributes to attenuate its magnitude. Based on this, two modulation techniques are proposed to not only minimize CMV, but also shape its frequency spectrum for further leakage current reduction. The first concentrates most of the energy around the switching frequency, making it suitable when the resonance frequency is significantly higher than switching frequency. The second redistributes a portion of the energy to higher-order harmonics, making it advantageous when the resonance frequency is close to or below the switching frequency. Both techniques support the full modulation index range and enable neutral-point (NP) voltage control. Simulations and experimental results validate their effectiveness across different resonance conditions. Capacitor voltages remain balanced even under a forced disturbance. In addition, an analysis of leakage current versus modulation index reveals an optimal range in which the current reaches minimal levels.

item.title: Enhanced AC-Side Startup Strategy of Hybrid Modular Multilevel Converters for Inrush Current Suppression and Capacitor Voltage Balancing
item.description: Hybrid modular multilevel converters (HMMCs) have emerged as a promising solution for high- and medium-voltage applications. However, during AC-side startup, HMMCs face challenges of the inrush current and the capacitor voltage imbalance between full-bridge submodules (FBSMs) and half-bridge submodules due to their structural differences. This article proposes a closed-loop black start strategy to address these issues. First, the analysis of the internal control loop dynamics reveals that, when submodule capacitors are at low voltage levels, the inrush current and the charging control failure may occur due to limited power regulation capability. An enhanced three-stage startup method is proposed to fully control the charging current with utilizing the negative voltage of FBSMs. With the proposed strategy, the inrush current during the precharging period is suppressed. The strategy is built upon a conventional controller framework with minimal additional modifications, significantly simplifying the overall design and implementation. Furthermore, supported by periodic approximation and piecewise calculation, it is demonstrated that the capacitor voltage imbalance can be inherently eliminated during the whole startup process. Finally, simulation and experimental results validate the theoretical analysis and the effectiveness of the proposed strategy.

item.title: Power Coordination for Electricity-Hydrogen DC Microgrids
item.description: It is common for DC microgrids (MGs) to have alkaline water electrolyzers (AWEs) embedded into the system. However, AWEs have low-voltage-high-current characteristics. As the voltage level and scale of electricity-hydrogen DC MGs expand, the traditional parallel-connected structure increases the requirement for the voltage conversion ratio of power electronic devices. Series connection of AWE units is a viable solution to this issue. Unfortunately, currently, there is a gap in the research on the series structure of AWEs. Focusing on this challenge, first, the electrothermal model of the AWEs is established. The model depicts the relationship between the electrolyzer’s maximum current and its temperature. On this basis, the topological structure of the multiseries connected AWEs module and its control strategy are proposed, which can not only reduce the input voltage level of each AWE unit but also achieve a reasonable power distribution among AWE units, and provide power support for DC MGs. Then, a small-signal model is established to evaluate the system stability using the proposed control. Finally, the findings are validated through a simple PV-AWE coupled DC MG experiment platform.

item.title: An Unfolder-Based Three-Phase AC–AC Converter
item.description: This article presents a three-phase AC–AC converter based on the unfolder topology, which consists of an unfolder rectifier and a T-type three-phase inverter connecting configuration. Since the converter inherits the features of the unfolder circuit such as ultra-low switching frequency and small DC-link capacitance, it exhibits high efficiency and high power density. Furthermore, it eliminates the additional balanced circuit or isolated DC–DC converters required in the conventional unfolder-based power converters, improving power density. To realize both power factor correction and output voltage regulation simultaneously, a special modulation scheme for the T-type three-phase inverter is proposed. The feasibility of the proposed converter is verified by theoretical derivation, simulation study, and experimental prototype testing.

item.title: Optimized Control for ISOP LLC-DAB Converter to Achieve Full-Power-Range Zero-Voltage Switching and Fast Dynamic Response
item.description: In recent years, the input-series-output-parallel LLC-DAB converter has been applied to DC microgrids and energy routers. However, the existing control methods still have some limitations, such as a limited range of soft switching, large current stress, and slow dynamic response speed, which restrict the efficiency and dynamic performance of the converter. This article optimizes the triple-phase-shift modulation scheme to achieve full-range soft-switching operation across varying input voltages, while simultaneously optimizing current stress characteristics and improving the converter's efficiency. Meanwhile, a more concise closed-loop control strategy is developed. Then, a direct power control strategy based on inductor current stress optimization is presented. This strategy calculates the phase-shift ratio parameters according to the converter's required power transmission. The calculation ensures the transmitted power quickly matches the load power. The approach improves the converter's dynamic performance during input voltage and load variations. The improvement is achieved while maintaining optimized current stress conditions. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed control strategy is verified by experimental results of a 2.5 kW prototype.

item.title: Time-Domain Modeling and Power Decoupling of the Cross-Coupled Active Power Flow in a Multi Active Bridge Converter
item.description: The principal component within the multi active bridge (MAB) topology is the high-frequency magnetic link (HFML) interconnecting multiple ports, thereby facilitating multidirectional power flow. However, a byproduct of this unifying AC magnetic link is the cross-coupled power flow induced by the inter-port phase shifts, subsequently preventing independent port-wise operation. Therefore, this work presents a time-domain analytical framework for the accurate estimation of the cross-coupled active power flow by applying a superposition-based approach to the MAB port-equivalent models. These port-equivalent models are derived from the full-order $n \times n$ inductance matrix ($\mathbf {L}$-matrix) extracted from an asymmetric integrated-leakage HFML via a single-port impedance measurement scheme. Furthermore, a control-integrated power decoupling (IPD) feedforward compensator is developed to suppress undesirable propagative voltage fluctuations during transient power flows, thereby ensuring port-wise decoupling of the converter. Steady-state experiments conducted on a 3.2 kW SiC-based four-port MAB prototype are used to validate the accuracy of the proposed time-domain model with respect to the terminal active powers. Whereas transient experiments with output-voltage step-changes are used to verify the improved decoupling performance of the proposed full-order $\mathbf {L}$-matrix IPD scheme, in comparison with an uncompensated proportional–integral (PI) controller, andthe existing $Y$-$\Delta$ (star-delta) decoupling method.

item.title: Reactive Components Analysis and Efficiency-Oriented Control for DAB Converters Under Variable Duty-Cycle Phase-Shift Modulation
item.description: The dual active bridge bidirectional dc–dc converter is critical in modern power conversion, requiring high efficiency across the entire power transmission range. Since reactive power does not transfer energy and only contributes to power loss, prior studies have proposed various reactive power minimization strategies to improve efficiency. However, most studies focus solely on the primary side and rely on fixed-duty-cycle phase-shift modulation, leaving the influence of the secondary side unclear and the potential of variable-duty-cycle phase-shift modulation [five-degree-of-freedom (5DoF)] unexplored. To address these gaps, this study analyzes both active and reactive power on both primary and secondary sides. Based on the analysis, under 5DoF modulation, a composite power factor maximization strategy, which simultaneously accounts for both sides, is proposed and validated through theoretical analysis and experimental comparison with existing methods. Compared with primary-side power factor maximization, the composite power factor maximization achieves lower rms current, a wider zero-voltage-switching region, and maintains high efficiency across the entire operating range, as verified by experimental results. At heavy load (normalized transmission power Ptn = 0.8), the proposed method achieves a maximum 96.0% efficiency, comparable to existing approaches, while at light load (Ptn = 0.1), it improves efficiency from 85.5% to 88.2%, thereby surpassing state-of-the-art techniques.

item.title: Design Optimization of Isolation Transformer for Synchronous Switching Common High Frequency AC Bus in MMC-PET
item.description: This article conducts a study on the DC–DC conversion channel, specifically emphasizing the high-frequency transformer in a power electronics transformer (PET). The PET is based on a modular multilevel converter (MMC) and features a common high-frequency AC bus configuration for the isolation stage, which plays a crucial role in its overall functionality and performance. By incorporating the parasitic parameters model of the high-frequency transformer, the current response, active power transfer, pulsating current transfer characteristics, voltage clamping characteristics, and the influence of common mode noise in the MMC-PET are analyzed. And then, the constraint of distributed parasitic parameters of the transformer is formed. The modeling of the distributed parameters is conducted while considering the parameters of magnetic core and windings. With the constraints aforementioned, the nondominated sorting genetic algorithm is used to optimize the transformer with multiple optimization objectives, including temperature rise, insulation voltage, transformer power density, efficiency, and parasitic capacitance. Finally, based on the optimization results, a transformer prototype with a rated power of 50 kW and an operating frequency of 10 kHz is designed. Experimental validation confirms the rationality of the optimized design.

item.title: Review of Power Electronic Solutions for Dielectric Barrier Discharge Applications
item.description: This article presents a comprehensive review of dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) power supply topologies, aiming to bridge the gap between DBD applications and power electronics design. Two key aspects are examined: the dependence of the DBD electrical model on reactor geometry and application-driven requirements for injected waveform characteristics, including shapes, voltage amplitude, frequency, and modulation techniques. On this basis, this article systematically reviews two major categories of power supplies: sinusoidal types comprising transformerless and transformer-based resonant inverters, and pulsed power supplies. The review summarizes performance tradeoffs, highlights untested topologies and emerging applications, and offers guidance for advancing high-performance DBD power supply design for next-generation systems.

item.title: Robust Stability Region Analysis of Weak-Grid-Following Converters With Multiple Uncertain Parameters
item.description: Robust stability region analysis of a voltage source converter under DC-link voltage control is essential to integrate inverter-based resources. The determination of operability specifications (e.g., control loops tuning boundaries) in weak grids becomes particularly challenging when accounting for simultaneous uncertainties in line impedance, transfer power, AC grid voltage, and DC-link capacitor. In this article, warranting the stability of the converter in uncertain contexts is formulated as a robust stability problem with structured singular value (also called μ). Gray-box modeling is adopted to account for the impact of variations in the power stage on the operating point by treating them as parametric uncertainties, whereas control loops tuning is modeled as parametric or unstructured uncertainties depending on the knowledge of the control structure. Robust stability margin is then efficiently identified as the smallest structured uncertainty matrix destabilizing the system, and the radical effect of the uncertainty on the robust stability region can be analytically quantified. A robust design with respect to μ results is proposed to guarantee a specific level of robustness. Exemplary results show a time saving of at least 90% compared to eigenvalue analysis without sacrificing accuracy. The performed analysis results are validated through the simulation and a lab-scale prototype.

item.title: Optimal Trajectory Control Scheme for Dual-Bridge Series-Resonant Converters With Constant and Small Low-Voltage Side Turn-Off Current
item.description: With the emerging of large-capacity battery cells, the demand of high-current, high-conversion-ratio bidirectional DC–DC converters in energy storage systems has also increased. Similarly, electric vehicle on-board chargers face the same requirement when charging or discharging low-voltage (LV) batteries. Although conventional approaches, such as inductor–inductor–capacitor converter, have provided good performance, their LV transistors still suffer from large switching stress during conversions from the LV side to the high-voltage side. In addition, due to the resonance, load transition response is slow and resonant amplitude oscillates during transients, which further increases the LV switching stress. To overcome these two shortcomings, an optimal trajectory control-based control for dual-active-bridge series-resonant converter is proposed. It uses state-plane analysis to find mode length solutions with the following two properties: First, for all the steady-state and transition solutions, zero-voltage switching (ZVS) always holds and the LV switching stress is constant and small; Second, transitions converge within one cycle and the resonant amplitude does not oscillate or overshoot. To validate the proposed solution, steady state tests, open-loop power transition tests, and closed-loop load transition tests are conducted on a 3-kW prototype. In all tests, ZVS and small turn-off current are observed in every cycle, which is consistent with expectations. Besides, single-cycle convergence and fast load transition response are also verified.

item.title: Reactive Power Control Strategy for Isolated Single-Stage Three-Phase SWISS AC–DC Converter Based on Dual Active Bridge Converters
item.description: This article proposes a reactive power control strategy for a three-phase single-stage isolated SWISS AC–DC converter based on the dual active bridge (DAB). By analyzing the conduction states of the SWISS converter, the relationship between the input currents of the two DAB converters and the grid current in different voltage sectors is examined. A triple phase shift (TPS) control strategy is applied to ensure the DAB converter works in the globally optimal working modes, thereby achieving zero-voltage switching (ZVS) of the high-frequency switching devices and effectively reducing conduction losses. Based on TPS control, an analytical expression relating the phase shift angle and the input current of the DAB converter is derived, with the sign of the phase shift angle determined according to the polarity of the grid current in each sector, thereby enabling precise control over both the magnitude and direction of the input current. A dual-loop control strategy is proposed to achieve reactive power control on the grid side and output regulation on the load side, thereby improving the power quality of the system. The variations in the working modes and control variables under the reactive power control strategy at different load powers are analyzed, demonstrating the strategy’s adaptability to a wide range of loads. In addition, the design method for the series inductor is analyzed to achieve reactive power control while meeting the load power requirements. Finally, the proposed control strategy is applied to a 1.4 kW three-phase single-stage SWISS converter based on DAB with 200 V/50 Hz input line voltage and 200 V output voltage. The experimental results validate the feasibility and effectiveness of the proposed control strategy.

item.title: LVRT Capability Enhancement of Grid-Forming Inverters Under Current Saturation: A Dual-Reference Dynamic Cooperative Control Strategy
item.description: While transient stability enhancement for grid-forming inverters (GFMs) considering the impact of current-reference limiter has been widely studied, it remains insufficiently explored for GFMs employing circular current limiter due to the heightened complexity of power-angle curves under this limiting scheme. Meanwhile, based on the timing of grid voltage sag and current saturation, there are two types of current saturation model (CSM) for GFMs. A unified approach enhancing low voltage ride-through (LVRT) capability across both CSMs has not been studied. To this end, this article proposes a dual-reference dynamic coordination control strategy for GFMs to enhance its LVRT capability. First, the explicit expression of output active power of GFMs incorporating dq-axis components of voltage reference is derived. Then, the transient synchronous stability control mechanism via power/voltage reference modulation is revealed by using equal area criterion and phase-portrait analysis. Second, based on the obtained stability mechanism, a dual-reference dynamic coordination strategy is proposed, which can automatically trigger mode-specific strategies for two types of CSM. It dynamically adjusts power reference to guarantee the existence of equilibrium points (EPs) and modulates voltage reference to ensure the EP is stable and maximize transient stability margin. Finally, experimental results verify the effectiveness of the proposed strategy.

item.title: Mechanism and Suppression of Mid-High Frequency Oscillation for Wind Farms Integrated With GFM-MMC System
item.description: Grid-forming control, due to its active support ability, emerges as a feasible method for renewable energy transmission. However, the oscillation characteristics of wind farms integrated with grid-forming modular multilevel converter (GFM-MMC) system have rarely been researched. This article reveals the mechanism of mid-high frequency oscillations in GFM-MMC system, and proposes a disturbance estimation-state prediction (DE-SP) based control method, which achieves delay compensation and disturbance suppression. First, the impedance model of wind farms and GFM-MMC considering frequency coupling effect is established, the impedance characteristics and oscillation mechanism are obtained. Subsequently, a DE-SP based control method is proposed, where a state estimator reconstructs system states, a filter estimates system disturbances, and a state predictor obtain future states. Then, the control structure of GFM-MMC is designed, and the impacts of control parameter on impedance characteristics is analyzed. Finally, the RTDS and compact RIO-based hardware-in-loop experimental is conducted. Compared with feedforward damping and Smith-based method, the proposed DE-SP method can not only suppress the mid- and high-frequency oscillations, but also exhibits strong robustness against disturbances and estimated delay compensation, which can significantly improve the stability of GFM-MMC system.

item.title: Torsional Vibrations Suppression for GFM-DFIG Wind Turbine Considering Electromechanical Interaction Effects
item.description: To effectively enhance the voltage and frequency supporting capabilities for power grid, the doubly fed induction generator based wind turbine (DFIG WT) can be connected to the grid via grid-forming (GFM) control. However, the GFM control intensifies the dynamic interaction between the mechanical and electrical system of DFIG WT, which exacerbates the risk of torsional vibrations. This article explains the underlying mechanism of TVs for GFM-DFIG WT through the active power loop model. Furthermore, the torque analysis is utilized to reveal the dominant factor of different control parameters on drivetrain damping. Results demonstrate that parameter adjustment alone cannot guarantee stability under ultraweak grid. For the sake of addressing this issue, the damping controller implementation optimization is conducted to maximize drivetrain damping of GFM-DFIG WT. Finally, the accuracy of the aforementioned conclusions is substantiated by experiment.

item.title: Model Predictive Control Based on Discrete Space-Vector Modulation for Single-Stage Dual-DC-Port Inverter
item.description: Model predictive control (MPC) provides an attractive solution for the single-stage dual-DC-port inverter (SDI) due to its capabilities to deal with multiobjective control under unbalanced DC-port voltages. However, the existing MPC for SDI uses only one of the basic voltage vectors throughout the control period, which increases the DC-port power ripple and reduces current quality. To address this issue, the candidate voltage vectors are extended from 27 to 117 based on unevenly distributed basic voltage vectors, which reduces the DC-port power ripple and improves power quality due to extra control degree of freedom being provided. To reduce the computational burden caused by extended voltage vectors under unbalanced port voltages, the space vector diagram is divided into asymmetrical quadrangle subsectors, where the candidate voltage vectors required to be iterated are reduced by 82$\%$. On this basis, an MPC based on discrete space-vector modulation (SVM) is proposed for the SDI, enabling simultaneous grid current tracking and flexible power regulation. The comprehensive comparison experimental tests are conducted to verify the effectiveness and advantages of the proposed MPC based on discrete SVM.

item.title: Real-Time Augmented Deep Reinforcement Learning Implementation Framework for Power Electronic Converters
item.description: Conventional deep reinforcement learning (DRL) frameworks for power electronics face several critical limitations, including nonreal-time training environments, poor generalization due to sim-to-real mismatches, inadequate thermal modeling, cumbersome controller deployment, and prolonged training durations. To address these challenges, this article proposes a real-time augmented DRL (RTA-DRL) framework that integrates the PLECS environment to enhance training fidelity and ensure real-time compatibility. Within this framework, the policy network is periodically deployed into PLECS hardware-in-the-loop (HIL), where real-time data are incorporated into the training process to strengthen policy robustness. The HIL stage further serves as an intermediate evaluation step to verify network execution and deployment feasibility on the target platform. An automated workflow based on a JavaScript Object notation - remote procedure call proxy enables seamless communication between MATLAB and PLECS. In addition, the framework incorporates the power switches' thermal model and parameter mismatch considerations in HIL evaluations to enhance generalization and bridge the sim-to-real gap. The proposed RTA-DRL is validated on a dual-active-bridge converter with triple-phase-shift control, targeting efficiency optimization under varying battery voltage conditions. Hardware experimentations demonstrate reduced training time, improved energy efficiency, and enhanced policy generalization of the proposed RTA-DRL framework.

item.title: Derivative-Free Grid Inertia Estimation via Frequency Ramp Test
item.description: With the significant decline in grid inertia over the past decade, accurate inertia estimation has become crucial to prevent frequency instability caused by insufficient inertia. Conventional measurement-based methods estimate grid inertia using the rate of change of frequency (RoCoF) during power step (P-Step) tests, where RoCoF is calculated using the derivative of the measured frequency. However, since derivative operation is highly sensitive to noise that is inevitably present in measured frequency, these methods cannot accurately estimate inertia and may even be invalid in high-noise environments. To address this issue, this article proposes a derivative-free grid inertia estimation method that replaces P-Step test with frequency ramp (f-Ramp) test. In f-Ramp test, the frequency varies in a ramp profile by regulating the power of converter-based sources. A key finding is that, unlike P-Step test, the grid inertia power during f-Ramp test is constant and its magnitude equals the constant component of converter power. Based on this characteristic, the proposed method estimates the grid inertia utilizing the aforementioned constant component instead of RoCoF, avoiding noise-sensitive derivatives. Then, the detailed procedure is introduced. Finally, the proposed method is validated through full-power hardware-based experiments, overcoming the limitations of simulation-based verification common in existing studies.

item.title: Enhancing Mode Transition Dynamics in Noninverting Buck-Boost Inverters for PV Systems
item.description: Quasi-single-stage inverters (QSSIs) are notable for their simple structure and bidirectional operation capability in applications such as photovoltaic (PV) systems. Among these QSSI, the noninverting buck-boost inverter or four-switch buck-boost inverter (FSBBI) is often preferred due to its ability to perform both step-down and step-up operations. However, when traditional control is used, achieving a smooth transition and efficient conversion becomes challenging as the output voltage approaches the input voltage. The pulsewidth ratio limitations and nonidealities of active and passive components are the cause of this. In this article, a comparative analysis of the mode transition techniques in FSBBI is presented using methods available for DC/DC converters. System efficiency and output voltage signal quality are selected as performance metrics. A 2-kW FSBBI is installed and controlled using single, two, modified two, three, and four-mode techniques. Simulation and experimental studies were conducted to validate the results. Based on these studies, the four-mode control technique was observed to be the most effective in eliminating dead zone effects, reducing total harmonic distortion (THD), and achieving the highest system efficiency in a PV system where a battery powers the AC load. Experimental results indicate that the four-mode modulation attained an efficiency of 95.49% with a THD of 2.97%.

item.title: A Geometric Large-Signal Stability Enhancement Method for GFLCs Under Symmetrical Faults
item.description: Large-signal synchronization instability of grid-following converters under symmetrical fault ride-through conditions is governed not only by saddle-node bifurcation (SNB) but also by homoclinic bifurcation (HB), which typically occurs prior to SNB and remains insufficiently addressed. This article shows that phase-locked loop (PLL) integrator antiwindup can postpone but cannot eliminate HB. A geometric stability enhancement method is, therefore, proposed to steer the system back to a pre-HB phase-space configuration through coordinated adaptive active-current correction and conditional damping, thereby enabling reliable resynchronization while preserving frequency-tracking capability during faults. The effectiveness of the proposed method is validated by dynamical-system analysis and comparative hardware experiments under severe symmetrical voltage sags.

item.title: An Advanced Energy-Coordinated Control for Half-Bridge Submodule-Based Centralized DC Chopper in Offshore Wind MMC-HVDC Systems
item.description: The half-bridge submodule (HBSM)-based centralized DC chopper (DCC) enables fault ride-through (FRT) in offshore wind power transmission systems. However, this solution suffers from SM capacitor overvoltage due to the unidirectional dissipation current. Existing modulation methods require additional SMs to achieve energy balancing, which increases investment costs. This article proposes an energy-coordinated control that utilizes the receiving-end converter (REC) as an energy buffer. The capacitor energy of the DCC is temporarily stored in the REC before the surplus power is dissipated. Therefore, without adding extra SMs, the peak SM capacitor voltage of the DCC can be kept within its rated value. To improve rapid DC-link voltage regulation, an energy-decoupled control scheme is adopted for the REC. This scheme is coordinated with a hysteresis-based control of the DCC to ensure stable management of REC's energy. A controller hardware-in-the-loop experiment based on the StarSim real-time platform is conducted to validate the proposed method. The experimental results demonstrate that the SM capacitor voltages of the DCC and the REC, the DC-link voltage, and the internal energy fluctuation of the REC are all maintained within allowable limits, thereby ensuring reliable and economic FRT operation.

item.title: An Efficient VF-PSD Modulation Scheme for CLLC Resonant Converter Featuring Full/Half-Bridge Compatibility and Optimized Frequency Range
item.description: The CLLC converter is an attractive option for battery charging applications due to their bidirectional power flow and soft-switching characteristics. However, the voltage gain curve of the CLLC converter is flat in the buck region, and existing variable-frequency or phase-shift variable-frequency modulation still require a wide switching frequency range, which increases the switching and core losses. To address this issue, a variable frequency - phase shift – duty cycle (VF-PSD) modulation is proposed in this article. The proposed modulation features narrow frequency range, full ZVS range, and lower RMS current by introducing asymmetric duty cycle control. Notably, when voltage gain M ≤ 0.5, the system automatically switches to half-bridge mode, further decreasing switching loss and conduction loss. A multiharmonic model is employed to indirectly analyze the time-domain characteristics of the CLLC converter under VF-PSD. Then, the optimal combinations of modulation variables are obtained through an offline particle swarm optimization algorithm. Finally, the advantages of VF-PSD modulation are verified through a 1-kW CLLC converter experimental prototype.

item.title: Mode-Switching Mechanism and Transient Stability Enhancement of Current-Limited GFM-DFIG Under Shallow Grid Faults
item.description: While capable of providing active support to the power grid, grid-forming doubly-fed induction generators (GFM-DFIGs) are susceptible to transient instability. Under shallow grid faults, a key observation is that the GFM-DFIG maintains operation in voltage source mode and subsequently switches to current source mode. This phenomenon presents two critical challenges for stability analysis: the requirement for a dual-port model that accurately depicts the transient power response, and the influence of reactive power controllers on this transition. To address this gap, this article develops a reduced-order transient model for GFM-DFIG that incorporates dual-port coupling characteristics. The effects of various reactive power controllers on both the mode-switching point and the transient response are systematically investigated. To address the tradeoff between steady-state reactive power requirement and transient stability enhancement, a reactive power controller switching strategy is introduced, along with the design of triggering thresholds for hysteresis control. Finally, control-hardware-in-loop simulation and full hardware experimental results collectively verify the accuracy of the theoretical analysis and the effectiveness of the proposed method.

item.title: Parallel Dual-DC-Port DC-AC Converters With Zero-Sequence Circulating Current Suppression Method for PV-Battery Hybrid Systems
item.description: A parallel structure of dual-DC-port DC–AC converters is proposed, which not only enables the simultaneous connection of photovoltaic and energy storage systems, but also features the advantages of high integration and efficiency. However, variations in hardware parameters or control strategies will introduce zero-sequence circulating current (ZSCC) between parallel loops, potentially leading to distorted output current and reduced system efficiency. Different from the conventional three-level parallel system, the coordination among multiport power control, modified carrier-based pulse width modulation (CB-PWM) scheme, and ZSCC suppression strategy is required in the dual-DC-port converter parallel system. Therefore, traditional ZSCC suppression methods are no longer applicable. To address the ZSCC issues in this parallel system, an equivalent model of ZSCC is first established, and then the generation mechanism of the ZSCC is analyzed in detail. A ZSCC suppression scheme combined with multiport power control is proposed for the parallel system. A modified CB-PWM strategy is adopted, in which asymmetrical carriers are employed to adapt to voltage variations. Meanwhile, zero-sequence voltage injection is utilized to achieve power control and ZSCC suppression. Experimental results validate the effectiveness of the proposed ZSCC suppression method for dual-DC-port converter parallel systems, and also verify the multiport power control range and efficiency.

item.title: A Decentralized Secondary Frequency Control Method for Maintaining Regulation Margin in Islanded Microgrids
item.description: Existing decentralized secondary frequency control methods can be mainly classified into two categories from the perspective of the actual regulation effect of the droop curve: intercept adjustment methods and slope adjustment methods. As the name implies, the intercept adjustment method restores system frequency by adjusting the intercept of the droop curve, which reduces the regulation margin of the distributed generators’ primary control in responding to subsequent load disturbances. On the other hand, the slope adjustment method restores system frequency by adjusting the slope of the droop curve. When the load power increases, the regulation margin of the primary control can be maintained unchanged by adopting this method. However, when the load power decreases, using this method will still reduce the regulation margin of the primary control. The decrease in regulation margin can adversely affect system stability and reliability. To address this issue, this article proposes a novel decentralized secondary frequency control method. In the proposed approach, only the slope is adjusted when the load demand increases, while both the intercept and slope are adjusted when the load demand decreases. As a result, the regulation margin of primary control can be preserved under any load variation conditions. In addition, this article establishes a synchronized adjustment mechanism and further investigates the case of consecutive application of the proposed method. The stability of the proposed method is analyzed, and its feasibility is verified through simulations and hardware experiments.

item.title: A Wide-Range Current Balanced Ultra-High-Gain Series-Capacitor Boost Converter With Symmetry-Integrated Step-Up Cells
item.description: Conventional ultra-high-gain series-capacitor boost converters typically employ common grounding of the input and output sides and switches, while achieving low voltage and current stresses. However, operating under symmetric control (i.e., equal duty cycles with a 360$^\circ$/m phase shift for m phases) with a duty cycle below a specific threshold (D$\leq$3/4 or 1/2, depending on the topology) leads to a loss of current balancing capability, decreased voltage gain, and increased switch voltage stress. To overcome these limitations, this article establishes the conditions for current balancing in two-phase boost-converter-based topologies. Based on this analysis, a method is proposed to extend the duty-cycle range for automatic current balancing while simultaneously enhancing the voltage gain, in which N-stage switched-capacitor or quasi-Z-source step-up cells are symmetrically integrated into both output diode branches of the conventional two-phase series-capacitor boost converter. The switched-capacitor configuration (N = 1) is taken as a representative example. Under symmetric control in continuous conduction mode (CCM) with D>1/3, the proposed converter maintains a constant voltage gain of 4/(1–D) with inherent inductor-current balancing. Moreover, the voltage stress of a single diode is limited to U$_{\mathrm{{o}}}$/2, whereas all remaining diodes and switches experience a reduced voltage stress of U$_{\mathrm{{o}}}$ /4. The operating principles and steady-state characteristics in CCM are comprehensively analyzed. In addition, small-signal models are developed together with controller design. A 24-60 V/500 W/50 kHz experimental prototype is built and tested, which demonstrates an inductor-current imbalance below 1% and a peak efficiency of 98% under CCM operation with D>1/3.

item.title: Frequency-Domain Methodology for Designing Load-Independent Class-E Power Converter With LCL and LCLC Resonant Network
item.description: This article presents a frequency-domain design method for load-independent (LI) Class-E power conversion, based on “Harmonic Voltage Analysis (HVA)”. Because of the complex resonant stage, the Class-E inverter with higher than two order resonant network (such as LCL and LCLC) cannot be addressed using the standard time domain method. An alternative approach is using frequency domain equations to characterize both the single switch invert and linear stages. The implementation of LI output voltage and LI zero voltage switching relies on careful design of the impedance and small-signal voltage gain in the high-order resonant network. In addition to the single-switch power inverter, the HVA method can be utilized to provide LI Class-E2 dc–dc conversion. Finally, a 24-V input-6.78-MHz-22.5-W LI Class-E inverter and a 24-V input-6.9-MHz-15-W LI Class-E2 dc–dc converter were fabricated and measured to verify the feasibility of the proposed HVA method.

item.title: Hybrid Modeling Approach Combining Analytical and Neural Ordinary Differential Equations for Accelerated Simulation of Grid-Tied Inverter
item.description: Fine solving time step is commonly required in the simulation of grid-tied inverters due to the fast inner-loop control and high-frequency switching behavior. However, the dynamics of the grid-tied inverters, especially in a large-scale renewable power farm study, are frequently governed by the slower outer-loop control. The mismatching between the small solving time step and the slow outer loop leads to time-consuming simulations when using conventional numerical methods. While a neural network (NN) can serve as a surrogate for the entire grid-tied inverter, modeling full behaviors still demands large and computationally intensive networks. To address this issue, this article proposes a hybrid modeling approach for accelerated simulation of the grid-tie inverters. It combines analytical models and neural ordinary differential equations (NODEs). NODE is employed to solely represent the inner loop dynamics, eliminating deep or complex networks, while the other components outside the inner loop are retained and analytically represented. This hybridization combines the coarse-step capability of NN-based surrogates with the physical interpretability and efficiency of analytical models. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed hybrid approach achieves high-fidelity predictions under various disturbances and grid faults, while significantly reducing simulation time compared with the conventional methods.

item.title: Optimal Design and Modulation of Full Bridge LLC Resonant Converter With Wide Voltage Range
item.description: Full bridge LLC resonant converters have become the mainstream solution in the field of wide voltage range due to their advantages, such as soft switching, simple structure, and wide voltage gain capability. To improve the working efficiency of the full bridge LLC resonant converters with a wide input voltage range, a working modes based iteration (WMI) modeling method is proposed to obtain both dynamic and steady-state waveforms, which exhibits high accuracy and speed, laying the foundation for the parameter and hybrid modulation strategy design. Then, with the purpose of minimizing the effective resonant current, a resonant cavity parameter design process is proposed based on traversing parameters with the WMI modeling method, thus guiding the selection of circuit key components. Finally, a loss model of the converter is built, and a hybrid optimization modulation strategy is proposed by taking the minimum total loss as the optimization objective. The accuracy and speed of the proposed WMI modeling method are verified by comparing with the MATLAB/Simulink result. With the designed parameters and hybrid modulation strategy, a 97.0% peak efficiency and a 4.8% maximum efficiency improvement are reached on a 125–375 V-input and 48 V/240 W-output prototype.

item.title: A Universal Automatic Optimal Design Method for CLLLC Class Resonant Converters and Comprehensive Comparison Among Different Resonant Tanks
item.description: CLLLC class resonant converters are widely applied in bidirectional power flow scenarios. However, the design of CLLLC is a complex process including the selection of resonant tanks, parameters, and magnetic cores together with various engineering restrictions. During the design process, time domain model (TDM) is widely used to improve the model accuracy, which contains multiple complex procedures and is hard to be applied. To further improve the universality and practicality of the existing design methods, an optimal design method for CLLLC is proposed in this article. Compared with the existing literatures, the following improvements are included in the proposed method: by developing the universal non-linear equations based on the symmetric condition, the operation mode judgement process of the TDM is eliminated, which significantly reduces the modeling complexity; the database-based magnetic core design process is included to conduct a multiobjective design including efficiency and power density, the engineering restrictions are also included in the process; and a hierarchical design flowchart of resonant tank parameters and magnetic related parameters is proposed to largely reduce the computational burden. Furthermore, by conducting the proposed design process for multiple typical applications, the pareto front of efficiency and power density are compared for different types of resonant tanks including asymmetrical CLLLC (ACLLLC) and CLLC. From the comparison results, ACLLLC and CLLC has identical electricity characteristic by using the parameter equivalence process, and CLLC has better efficiency and power density performance. The design process and comparison results are verified by a 1 kW prototype with three types of resonant tanks, which shows that CLLC has higher efficiency compared with ACLLLC, while the total volume of magnetic cores can be reduced from 32.42 cm3 to 26.98 cm3.

item.title: Challenges and Opportunities in Electric Propulsion Systems for Low-Altitude eVTOL Aircraft
item.description: Low-altitude aviation is rapidly emerging as a transformative solution for next-generation transportation, driving significant advancements in electric propulsion systems. As a core subsystem in electric aircraft, electric propulsion systems require comprehensive evaluation of their technological progress, ongoing challenges, and future opportunities to support the development of low-altitude aviation. Unlike land vehicle applications, aircraft propulsion systems demand safety-critical designs governed by stringent operational constraints. This review identifies three critical metrics for such systems: power density, operational reliability and fault tolerance, and thermal management efficiency. While existing studies often address these metrics individually, their interdependencies are not well explored. To bridge this gap, this review systematically investigates state-of-the-art solutions, benchmarks their limitations under flight-critical conditions, and establishes correlations between technological advancements and operational requirements. Furthermore, it highlights future research directions by identifying emerging methodologies. As low-altitude aviation approaches scalable deployment, this article aims to serve as a technical reference to support the safe and effective development of aerial propulsion technologies.

item.title: $LCC\text{--}S\text{--}S$ Double-Coupling Resonant Converter for In-Wheel Dynamic IPT Charging
item.description: This work presents an iterative design methodology based on the equivalent mathematical model of a resonant converter for in-wheel dynamic inductive power transfer (IPT) applications. This design methodology considers the magnetic coupling dimensions as well as the resonant circuit parameters that achieve resonant operation and meet dynamic requirements. The system uses the wheel as an intermediary stage to transfer energy into the vehicle, using two magnetic couplers (MC), maintaining a minimal air gap throughout the energy transfer process. The first MC transfers power from the off-board transmitters to the receiver in the wheel and the second MC transfers the power from the wheel to the electric vehicle. The design methodology ensures load independent transconductance gain from a double coupling $LCC\text{--}S\text{--}S$ resonant converter. In this way, the proposed system offers a low cost solution using an open-loop control and without feedback requirements from the vehicle. The iterative design methodology combines the self and mutual inductance values and coupling factor profiles of both MCs, obtained from a finite element analysis (FEA) tool, taking into consideration the system specifications and constraints to find all possible solutions. A set of selection metrics are then proposed to identify the optimal solution. An experimental prototype was built to validate the safe operation under full range of operating conditions in dynamic applications. An overall dc–dc efficiency of approximately 78% when transferring 2 kW to the load was achieved. In dynamic operation and, considering one transmitter pad, a maximum energy transfer of 27 Wh is achieved when moving at a speed of 30 m/h.

item.title: Generalized Input Ripple Characterization Using Inductor Current Behavioral Pattern for Interleaved Boost Converter in Discontinuous Conduction Mode
item.description: Interleaved DC–DC converters [interleaved boost converter (IBCs)] are widely utilized in high-power applications because they minimize input and output current ripples, enhancing the performance and lifespan of connected components. While ripple characteristics in continuous conduction mode) are well-established, a comprehensive analysis of discontinuous conduction mode (DCM) of input ripples is less explored. This article presents a generalized ripple minimization and characterization methodology in N-phase IBCs operating in DCM. The proposed approach provides a detailed analysis of input ripple characteristics across various operating points and configurations, addressing the limitations of previous works that focus solely on inductor ripple. By deriving generalized formulas, this methodology enables accurate computation of total ripple for any phase count, operating point, and inductor value. This analysis is pivotal for optimizing filter designs, reducing thermal stress, and improving the overall efficiency of converters. Furthermore, voltage gain expression is also derived for any number of phases, operating points, and inductor values. Overall, the proposed framework also facilitates early-stage design, evaluation of ripple mitigation algorithms, and accurate assessment of converter losses. The results demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed approach in achieving ripple minimization across a wide range of conditions, offering significant insights for advanced IBC design.

item.title: Fractional-Order Terminal Sliding Mode Control Based Model Predictive Control of Totem-Pole PFC for EV Charging Applications
item.description: This article proposes an advanced control strategy for a totem-pole bridgeless boost power factor correction (TPB-PFC) converter, combining fractional-order terminal sliding mode controller (FOTSMC) in the outer voltage control loop with model predictive control (MPC) in the inner current control loop. Traditional PFC converters use cascaded proportional–integral controllers, which struggle with performance under disturbances due to their linear nature. While sliding mode control (SMC) offers improved robustness, its application in voltage regulation is limited by bandwidth constraints. To address these challenges, a more continuous FOTSMC is proposed that enhances dynamic performance and robustness by introducing fractional-order dynamics and disturbance rejection features. The MPC ensures optimal switching decisions, further improving efficiency and transient response. In addition, expanding the bandwidth of the voltage controller may not sufficiently cancel out the double frequency component contained in the output voltage, causing this component to affect the current reference and ultimately induce third harmonic distortion in the input current. To prevent these issues, a moving average filter is used to remove the double frequency component from the output voltage, thereby suppressing the harmonic distortion in the input current. The combined control framework is validated through experiments under various test cases.

item.title: Voltage Gain-Based Control for Four-Switch Buck-Boost Converter Exhibiting Fast Dynamic Response Over Wide Output Voltage and Load Range
item.description: The four-switch buck–boost converter combines zero-voltage switching (ZVS) capability with both step-up and step-down voltage conversion, making it ideal for applications that require a wide voltage range and high efficiency. However, conventional ZVS control methods rely on three mutually coupled modulation variables, which increases implementation complexity and complicates controller design. The interdependence of these variables demands careful coordination of their dynamic responses during transients to maintain optimal ZVS performance, as any mismatch can degrade transient response and stability margins. To address these challenges, this article proposes a voltage gain-based control strategy that synchronizes the dynamic changes of the modulation variables, significantly reducing settling time and improving output voltage regulation across a wide range of output voltages and load conditions. Furthermore, the proposed method ensures ZVS operation without the need for analog circuitry, thereby enhancing both reliability and implementation simplicity. The effectiveness of the proposed strategy is validated using a 140 W, 100 kHz GaN-based prototype, with experimental results demonstrating that the converter settles within several switching cycles under varying load and voltage conditions and achieves a peak efficiency of 98.5% across the entire operating range.

item.title: A Unified Approach to Open-Circuit and Short-Circuit-Fault Detection and Localization in Three-Phase Dual Active Bridge Converters
item.description: This article presents a unified, single-sensor-based method for detecting and localizing both open-circuit faults (OCFs) and secondary-side short-circuit faults (SCFs) in three-phase dual-active-bridge (TP-DAB) converters using the $\alpha \beta$ framework. In the proposed approach, the existing current-sense resistor, traditionally used for output-current regulation, is repositioned to measure the unfiltered DC-link current, from which the required $\alpha \beta$ parameters are extracted. This eliminates the need for additional current or voltage sensors, thereby reducing system complexity and cost. A ${\text{400}} \,$-${\text{V}}$, ${\text{6.5}} \,$-${\text{k}}$${\text{W}}$ gallium nitride (GaN)-based TP-DAB prototype, operating at $300 \,\mathrm{k}\mathrm{Hz}$ and achieving a peak efficiency of 96.6%, was developed to validate the proposed method. Experimental results demonstrate that single-switch OCFs are detected and localized within $13 \,\mu$s (four switching cycles), while SCFs are detected within $268 \,\mathrm{n}\mathrm{s}$—well within the short-circuit withstand time of the tested GaN devices. Thus, the proposed algorithm offers a practical and hardware-efficient solution for improving the reliability of onboard chargers.

item.title: Power Control of a Capacitively Isolated Bidirectional DC–DC Converter With Auxiliary Converters
item.description: This article verifies an improved control method for a capacitively isolated bidirectional DC–DC converter with auxiliary converters, which is the first design intended for electric railway systems. An inner power control method based on triangular inductor currents adjusted by a feedforward voltage is proposed. The operating principles of the proposed control are validated experimentally using a 1.2-kW, 120-V down-scaled model, where the main converter operates at 4 kHz, and through a 330-kW, 3-kV simulation model that demonstrates reverse power flow. The measured efficiency of the down-scaled model reaches 91.51% at 1.2 kW.

item.title: Symmetric Unipolar Pulsewidth Modulation for Dual-Active-Bridge Converters
item.description: This article proposes a novel symmetric unipolar modulation (SUM) strategy for dual-active-bridge converters, which significantly enhances power density by improving transformer utilization factor and reducing the size of passive components. The proposed SUM technique effectively doubles the transformer frequency ($f_{\text{TX}}$) to $2f_{\text{sw}}$ while maintaining the switching frequency ($f_{\text{sw}}$) of both the primary- and secondary-side bridge switches at $f_{\text{sw}}$, unlike conventional methods where the semiconductor and transformer frequencies are equal. By pushing the transformer frequency to a higher level, the proposed technique minimizes passive component dimensions without introducing additional stress on the active or passive components. Moreover, the SUM approach reshapes the transformer core flux density from triangular to trapezoidal while reducing its peak and enabling further reduction in magnetic core size. It also reduces the voltage stress across the transformer by half compared to conventional modulation strategies, thereby improving the overall performance of the converter. Experimental validation using a 1-kW prototype confirms the effectiveness of the proposed modulation technique in achieving these benefits.

item.title: Variable Dual Phase-Shift DPWM of ZVS Paralleled Inverters With Optimal Current Stress for AC Motor Drive
item.description: Zero voltage switching (ZVS) control is an effective approach for paralleled inverters with coupled inductors in AC motor drives, as it enhances switching frequency, efficiency, and overall control performance. However, conventional ZVS paralleled inverters only utilize the freedom of zero-sequence injection to adjust switching frequency, which faces critical frequency conflicts for different phases, resulting in high current stress and suboptimal power capacity utilization. To address this issue, the freedom of phase-shift operation for paralleled phase-legs is implemented in this article, and a variable dual phase-shift (VDPS) method under the discontinuous pulsewidth modulation (DPWM) is proposed for ZVS paralleled inverters. In the proposed VDPS-DPWM method, the circulating current ripple prediction model is proposed with the real time phase-shift angle, allowing the reconstruction of phase-leg current trajectories through the superposition of sampled fundamental current and predicted circulating current ripple. Additionally, an optimized VDPS angle is derived to mitigate critical frequency conflicts. The proposed VDPS-DPWM method demonstrates improved ZVS control and significant current stress optimization across the entire operational range. Simulation and experimental results are provided to validate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

item.title: An IPT-CPT Hybrid Wireless Power Transfer System for Uncrewed Aerial Vehicles
item.description: In the field of uncrewed aerial vehicle (UAV) wireless charging, inductive power transfer (IPT) and capacitive power transfer (CPT) have become the two main technical approaches. However, existing single-topology systems still face significant challenges. This article proposes a hybrid IPT-CPT wireless power transfer (WPT) system to address key limitations of lightweight design and landing misalignment tolerance. First, a hybrid coupler is designed, with CPT serving as the main energy transfer channel. By reusing the compensation inductor as the coupler of IPT, the integration level of the system is improved. Then, a frequency-variable reconfiguration strategy with single-switch control is introduced to enable constant current (CC) and constant voltage (CV) output modes. Furthermore, a modified MOEA/D algorithm is employed to co-optimize electromagnetic parameters, balancing transmission efficiency and output power. Experimental results demonstrate that the system delivers 218.8 W with 86.38% efficiency in CC mode at 750 kHz and 216.6 W with 81.39% efficiency in CV mode at 778 kHz. The system exhibits strong misalignment tolerance, maintaining stable operation within $\boldsymbol{\pm}$25 mm (x-axis) and $\boldsymbol{\pm}$100 mm ($y$-axis) offset ranges.

item.title: Design and Optimization of the Cross-Frequency Negative Permeability and Near-Zero Permeability Metasurface for WPT System
item.description: Metasurface provides a promising solution to address the efficiency-distance-safety challenges in magnetic coupling resonant wireless power transfer (MCR-WPT) systems through subwavelength electromagnetic field manipulation. Current research primarily focuses on single-frequency operation or mostly MHz-band applications. It lacks theoretical and simulated studies of kHz band, let alone the cross-frequency band (both kHz and MHz simultaneously). In this article, a cross-frequency metasurface in the MCR-WPT system has been proposed. First, an equivalent circuit model of a cross-frequency metasurface has been derived. Then, EMF analysis of WPT system based on the cross-frequency metasurface has been presented. Particularly, an improved dual-population cooperative evolutionary algorithm has been employed to optimize both cross-frequency mu-negative (CF-MNG) and cross-frequency mu-near-zero (CF-MNZ) metasurfaces. Finally, the cross-frequency metasurface exhibits excellent performance on efficiency improvement and EMF leakage reduction in MCR-WPT system. The system with CF-MNG loading achieved nearly double the transfer efficiency at both 350 kHz and 6.78 MHz frequencies. Additionally, the decline of leakage electromagnetic field (EMF) with CF-MNZ metasurface can reach up to 2.42 and 6.51 dB at two frequencies, respectively. Therefore, this article not only provides significant guidance for low-frequency metasurface design but also offers potential for future applications in simultaneous power and information transfer systems.

item.title: Prioritized Expert-Based Multi-Agent Deep Reinforcement Learning for Eco-Driving of Plug-In Hybrid Electric Vehicles: A Model Predictive Control Framework
item.description: Integrating adaptive cruise control with energy management strategy represents a promising approach to improving the energy efficiency of plug-in hybrid electric vehicle within the domain of eco-driving. This article proposes a novel model predictive control (MPC) eco-driving framework based on multi-agent deep deterministic policy gradient (MADDPG). First, a MADDPG model is developed for car-following scenario to jointly optimize vehicle speed and power distribution. An expert-guided priority experience replay mechanism is incorporated to enhance agent training efficiency and overall performance. Subsequently, a leading-vehicle speed predictor is constructed using a Transformer bi-directional long short-term memory-based network. Second, the trained agents are integrated into the MPC framework to enable multi-step predictive optimization of the control sequence within the time horizon. Finally, comparative experiments under real-world driving cycles demonstrate that the proposed strategy outperforms state-of-the-art multi-agent deep reinforcement learning methods in terms of efficiency, optimization capability, and adaptability. Its practical feasibility is further validated through hardware-in-the-loop testing.

item.title: Autonomous Gate Drivers for TCM-Based Soft-Switched Converters: Design Approach and Experimental Validation
item.description: This article presents a soft-switched buck converter employing autonomous gate drivers (AGDs) for high-performance power electronic systems. Operating at a 400 V dc-link, representative of electric vehicle (EV) and industrial applications, the converter achieves zero-voltage switching (ZVS) during both turn-on and turn-off through fully analog AGD circuitry and optimized snubber capacitance. The converter operates in triangular current mode (TCM), leveraging the inherent inductor current ripple to enable ZVS without digital control. Experimental results demonstrate reliable soft switching, reduced switching losses, and effective suppression of voltage overshoot under realistic operating conditions. Although validated using a buck converter, the proposed AGD concept is directly applicable to more complex topologies, including three-phase inverters with sinusoidal reference currents, relevant to EV traction drives, renewable energy systems, and industrial converters. This work highlights a scalable, hardware-efficient approach for improving efficiency and performance in next-generation high-voltage power converters.

item.title: Data-Driven Speed Synchronization Predictive Control of a Five-Leg VSI Driving Dual-PMSM System
item.description: Precise speed synchronization control of dual-motor and multimotor systems is essential in specific industrial applications. This article studies an improved data-driven speed synchronization predictive control (DSSPC) scheme of dual permanent magnet synchronous motors (PMSMs) powered by a five-leg inverter. Based on characteristic modeling theory, an equivalent first-order model is established for current prediction without motor parameter dependence. An improved model predictive control (MPC) with duty cycle adjustment is proposed, considering the switching constraint from the common leg. A new global cost function is formulated for the selection of optimal voltage vectors (VVs) of dual PMSMs. Moreover, a current reference compensation method is introduced to replace the traditional cross-coupling control with constant gain. It can quickly eliminate the synchronization error between dual PMSMs against disturbances. An extensive comparison with experimental tests is conducted between the proposed DSSPC scheme and the existing advanced approaches. The results prove that the proposed DSSPC strategy concurrently mitigates speed synchronization error and tracking error effectively, and with higher robustness against parameter mismatches and external disturbance.

item.title: Feedforward-Based Variable Frequency Second Harmonic Current Suppression Strategy for Cascaded H-Bridge Energy Storage Converter
item.description: The second harmonic current (SHC) is significant in the cascaded H-bridge converter with dc–dc stage and supercapacitor under high current and variable output frequency, increasing the risk of overcurrent in the dc–dc. Conventional suppression strategies designed for fixed-frequency SHC constrain the dc–dc controller bandwidth to the SHC frequency, inevitably degrading dynamic performance. To address this issue, this article proposes a feedforward-based SHC suppression strategy. By analyzing the transmission path of SHC, an accurate feed-forward compensation is introduced at the dc voltage reference point, blocking the second harmonic signal from entering the dc–dc control loop. This decouples the SHC suppression strategy from the controller, eliminating the bandwidth constraint imposed by the SHC frequency. Therefore, the proposed strategy ensures both effective SHC suppression and enhanced dynamic performance under variable frequency conditions. Furthermore, the dc–dc controller design process is significantly simplified. Finally, the experimental results validate the effectiveness of the proposed SHC suppression strategy.

item.title: Frequency-Splitting-Free Wireless Power Transfer via Hybrid Frequency Control and Asymmetric Voltage Cancellation
item.description: In this article, a hybrid frequency control and asymmetric voltage cancellation (HFC-AVC) strategy is presented to address frequency-splitting challenges in wireless power transfer (WPT) systems. Although WPT systems with varying operating frequencies offer advantages in hardware simplicity, existing frequency control approaches suffer from the frequency-splitting phenomenon. The proposed HFC-AVC strategy is developed to tackle this issue. First, the frequency-splitting phenomenon is analytically investigated under detuned conditions, and a criterion is established to determine the critical load resistance that triggers splitting. Based on this, the constant current-constant voltage (CC-CV) battery charging process is divided into two regions: the frequency-splitting region and the frequency-splitting-free region. In the frequency-splitting region, constant frequency control is adopted, where output regulation is achieved through AVC modulation, while zero-voltage switching (ZVS) is maintained via an optimized detuning strategy. Conversely, in the frequency-splitting-free region, variable frequency control is applied, with output regulation achieved by tuning the frequency. The proposed approach enables wide-range output regulation across the entire CC-CV process while maintaining full-range ZVS, without requiring any additional hardware circuits. More importantly, it overcomes frequency splitting challenges. Experimental results confirm that the proposed strategy effectively achieves CC-CV charging with full-range ZVS, and attains a peak dc-to-dc efficiency of 96.34%.

item.title: Series Hybrid Flexible Traction Substation for AC Electric Railways With Reduced Converter Capacity
item.description: The flexible traction power system (TPS) is a key development trend for ac electric railways. However, the substantial converter investment cost of its core component, the flexible traction substation (FTSS), limits its widespread application. To reduce this cost, this article proposes a series hybrid FTSS (SFTSS), whose power converter handles only a fraction of the total load power. Because the SFTSS retains the existing traction transformer, it can seamlessly integrate into widely used TPSs that operate at the utility grid frequency. Based on analyzing the working principle, internal power flow, and negative sequence current (NSC) characteristics of the SFTSS, a biobjective control method is designed to stabilize dc-bus voltages and suppress emitted NSC. Furthermore, an average consensus-based distributed coordinated voltage control method is proposed to ensure efficient, stable networked operation of multiple SFTSSs. Hardware-in-the-loop tests were conducted to verify the effectiveness of the proposed SFTSS and control methods. Moreover, existing FTSSs with a 30 MVA load capacity typically require a total converter capacity of 60 MVA. In contrast, the proposed SFTSS requires only 31.46 MVA, achieving a 47.56% reduction, which significantly reduces investment cost while maintaining comparable performance in NSC suppression and load supply.

item.title: Embedded Broadband EIS Measurement System Based on Lithium-Ion Battery DC–DC Converter
item.description: Accurate, online wide-frequency electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement is essential for real-time state estimation and early fault detection of lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries. However, conventional online EIS methods rely on external signal generation equipment and are limited by the bandwidth of traditional proportional-integral (PI) controllers, causing high-frequency distortion and low accuracy. Therefore, an embedded EIS measurement system based on Li-ion battery dc–dc converter is proposed, featuring a duty ratio compensation PI (DRC-PI) control strategy. By designing a duty ratio function and decoupling EIS measurement from charging control through digital filtering, the proposed method achieves accurate, real-time wide-frequency EIS measurements without additional hardware. Experimental results show that, compared to traditional PI control, the DRC-PI control extends the EIS frequency range from 0.1 Hz–2.8 kHz to 0.1 Hz–25 kHz, reduces the maximum normalized root mean square errors (RMSEs) from 6.32% to 1.56%, and shortens the measurement time from 448.43 to 60.78 s compared with commercial electrochemical workstations. These improvements verify the method’s potential for embedded, high-precision battery diagnostics and online safety management.

item.title: Lifetime Enhanced Modulation Method for Unbalanced Loss Issue in Sustainable Renewable Converters
item.description: The increasing power losses in renewable energy converters can accelerate the degradation of power modules and reduce system reliability. This article proposes an enhanced modulation method to address the unbalanced loss distribution in typical renewable converter modules. The approach periodically adjusts switching operation modes to equalize power losses and average the junction temperature of power devices. A full-bridge inverter module is used to demonstrate the method, and experiments confirm that it achieves a more balanced loss distribution compared with traditional modulation. Reliability analysis based on Weibull function-derived time-to-failure distributions and annual mission profiles of a 5 kW photovoltaic (PV) system in Arizona shows that the proposed method can significantly extend the lifetime of power modules without increasing system cost. Furthermore, life cycle assessment (LCA) shows that the proposed method prevents at least two module replacements over a 25-year service life, reducing environmental impact. By improving module longevity, the method also contributes to the overall sustainability of renewable converters.

item.title: A Symmetrical Component Decomposition-Based Unified Protection Technique for Hybrid AC/DC Microgrid
item.description: Hybrid ac/dc microgrids (MGS) allow seamless integration of various renewable energy sources with unique fault characteristics of the two subgrids, mandating a resilient unified protection approach. This article proposes a unified scheme for the detection and classification of ac/dc faults based on the symmetrical component decomposition of line currents. The symmetrical domain currents are utilized for the computation of symmetrical component voltage drops (VDs) across the line section (both at the near and far end). The superimposed positive sequence VD (balanced component) plotted in the four-quadrant $\boldsymbol{\Delta V}_{\boldsymbol{d1}}$ plane helps detect various internal fault conditions of the system. Simultaneously, the superimposed negative sequence VDs (unbalanced component) are employed to classify the fault type, enhancing the system’s reliability and continuity of supply. Additionally, the vulnerability of the protection schemes to false data injection based cyber intrusions is addressed through rapid detection based on zero sequence (bias component) of the line VDs. The initial triggering of the protection algorithm is performed by estimating the fault energy of the line currents using empirical mode decomposition. The efficacy of the proposed protection scheme is validated for the 11-bus ac/dc hybrid MG test system. The test system is developed in MATLAB/Simulink and is based on a modified MV CIGRE benchmark MG system. The results manifest the superiority of the developed scheme in various fault and nonfault scenarios. The proposed scheme is finally validated in a hardware-in-the-loop simulation environment using a real-time digital simulator and a dSPACE DS1104 DSP controller.

item.title: Robust Dual Loop Nonlinear Controller for Four-Phase Interleaved Boost Converter in Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle Applications
item.description: This article presents a robust dual-loop nonlinear controller for a four-phase interleaved boost converter (FP-IBC) in fuel cell electric vehicle (FCEV) applications. Regulating the dc bus voltage at the output of converters in FCEVs is challenging due to the frequently varying fuel cell (FC) output and changing vehicle load demands. To address this, impulsive adaptive supertwisting sliding mode control (IASTSMC) is designed for the inner current loop of the FC, while adaptive supertwisting sliding mode control (ASTSMC) is proposed for the outer voltage loop to generate the reference current for the inner loop. The finite-time stability of the system with the proposed controller is proven using the Lyapunov stability criterion. The controller’s performance is evaluated through extensive testing, including load changes, variations in input voltage, and variations in the desired output voltage. For comparative analysis, state-of-the-art techniques such as proportional-integral (PI) control, model predictive control (MPC), backstepping-supertwisting sliding mode control (BS-STSMC), and dual-loop supertwisting sliding mode control (DL-STSMC) are designed and implemented. Simulation results obtained using MATLAB/Simulink demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed controller compared to benchmark methods, achieving faster response, lower overshoot, and reduced chattering. Furthermore, hardware experiments on the Imperix platform demonstrate the proposed controller’s practical feasibility and superior performance, highlighting its advantages for FCEV applications.

item.title: An Improved Reactance Control for Stability Enhancement of Grid-Forming Converter Based on Series-Regulated Branch
item.description: Virtual admittance control can enhance the stability of grid-forming converters in stiff grid. However, admittance control may induce a negative damping effect during reverse power operation, leading to system instability. Furthermore, under low dc-link voltage conditions, grid-forming converters implementing virtual admittance control are prone to over-modulation, resulting in control failure. To address these issues, this article proposes an improved reactance control that adjusts the set reactance to compensate for the deviation in equivalent reactance based on the frequency difference.
